meta.vintage: Stats 2025 season; staff/QB notes web-verified 2026-07-13 (top 25 + full SEC)

Indiana.ob: Zone-RPO spread under Curt Cignetti — 11 personnel, condensed splits, heavy play-action and glance RPOs off wide zone.
Indiana.ot: Methodical by design: elite efficiency (#4 success rate) with almost no vertical variance (#114 explosiveness). Best red-zone offense in the country (#1 points/opportunity). QB room resets after Fernando Mendoza's departure — the system, not the passer, is the constant.
Indiana.db: 4-2-5 under Bryant Haines — aggressive simulated pressures, run-through linebackers, press-quarters behind it.
Indiana.dt: #1 stuff rate and #2 havoc in FBS: they win at the line and on early downs. The tradeoff is boom-bust — #108 at preventing explosives. Teams that protect and take shots have a path; nobody grinds them down.
s: Best situational football in the sport — red zone and 3rd down on both sides
Coaching edge: Cignetti's staff wins the margins
w: Explosiveness allergy on offense caps blowout upside vs elite defenses
New QB after Mendoza — efficiency machine needs a new trigger man
Secondary can be beaten over the top when pressure doesn't land

Ohio State.ob: Pro-spread under Ryan Day with new OC Arthur Smith (ex-Falcons HC/Steelers OC, hired after Brian Hartline left for the USF job) — expect more 12/13 personnel, play-action, and a real run-game commitment layered onto the WR pipeline.
Ohio State.ot: Julian Sayin returns behind Jeremiah Smith and Carnell Tate — the highest-ceiling pass game in the country, now with Smith's NFL wide-zone/keeper structure around it. The 2025 season ended in a Cotton Bowl CFP loss; the offense, not the defense, was the reason.
Ohio State.db: Multiple front under Matt Patricia — disguised coverage shells, NFL-style game-planning, elite pass defense metrics.
Ohio State.dt: Top-5 in EPA allowed and pass efficiency defense in 2025. They rally-tackle and squeeze explosives; pressure comes from scheme more than raw blitzing.
s: Sayin-to-Smith is the best QB-WR axis in the sport
Back-to-back elite defenses with NFL scheme diversity
Two former NFL head coaches now coordinate both sides
w: First-year college OC install — NFL-to-CFB transitions are not automatic
OL play, not skill talent, decides their CFP ceiling
Run-game identity was still optional against elite fronts in 2025

Notre Dame.ob: Duo/counter downhill run game under Mike Denbrock — 12/13 personnel mixed with CJ Carr's rhythm play-action.
Notre Dame.ot: Even post-Jeremiyah Love, the identity is RB-led: gap-scheme runs, heavy sets, then max-protect shots. Carr showed a live arm as a first-year starter; they'll trust him more in 2026.
Notre Dame.db: Tite-front quarters-match — sound, positionally disciplined, built to erase explosives first.
Notre Dame.dt: Bend-don't-break with elite tackling. They trade some havoc for structure; screens and QB run are the historical soft spots.
s: Carr's year-two leap is the highest-probability QB improvement in the top 10
Freeman's roster has no bottom-quartile position group
w: Independent schedule means zero bad-week amnesty
WR separation vs elite man coverage is still a question
Pass rush relies on scheme more than a dominant edge

Oregon.ob: Lanning's spread — wide zone base with layered RPOs and motion at the conference's highest rate — now under a new play-caller after OC Will Stein left to become Kentucky's head coach.
Oregon.ot: Dante Moore distributes to a rebuilt-but-elite skill room. They sequence runs into shot plays better than anyone in the Big Ten; 3rd-and-medium is where Stein's script lives.
Oregon.db: 4-2-5 rebuilt on both coordinator chairs: Will Stein (OC) left for the Kentucky job and DC Tosh Lupoi took the Cal job — Lanning's system continuity is the through-line.
Oregon.dt: The scheme (movement fronts, deep DL rotation, explosive-play denial) is Lanning's more than any coordinator's, but two first-year play-callers is real friction — verify both hires before air.
s: Best roster construction in the Big Ten — portal and HS classes both top-5
Special teams and field position consistently elite
w: Have not won the biggest CFP-stage game yet under Lanning
Interior DL vs power run games (see: physical B1G losses)
Stein's exit means a new voice calling it for Dante Moore

Miami.ob: Shannon Dawson's one-back spread — tempo, quick game to the boundary, vertical seams — behind Cristobal's mauling OL.
Miami.ot: The portal answer arrived: Darian Mensah from Duke — the ACC's leading passer (3,973 yds, 34/6 TD-INT) who won the league title against them. Pair that with a top-10 line-yards OL and this is the ACC's most complete offense on paper.
Miami.db: Corey Hetherman's attack front — high sim-pressure rate, aggressive run fits, man-heavy behind it.
Miami.dt: Big havoc jump in 2025; they trade structure for negative plays. Vertical shots off play-action are the counterpunch opponents live on.
s: Elite OL/DL two-deep — Cristobal recruits the trenches like nobody in the ACC
Mensah is the conference's best returning QB resume
2025 CFP run (Fiesta Bowl win over Ole Miss) broke the January seal
w: Aggression = explosives allowed when pressure whiffs
Mensah's first year in a new building — Duke's spacing isn't Dawson's tempo
Expectations now assume CFP; anything less reopens the Cristobal questions

Texas Tech.ob: Mack Leftwich's tempo spread — Air-Raid DNA with a real run game, top-25 explosiveness, fastest-scripting offense in the Big 12.
Texas Tech.ot: Behren Morton's exit hands the keys to a portal/blue-chip competition. They push tempo (73.9 plays/g) and hunt shots; success rate (#61) lags the highlight reel — drive-finishing came from defense-gifted fields.
Texas Tech.db: Shiel Wood's 3-3-5 — tite front, pressure from every second-level angle. #1 EPA defense, #1 success rate allowed, #1 havoc in 2025.
Texas Tech.dt: The best defense money could build: bought the entire DL two-deep from the portal and it worked. They win 1st down, then tee off. Question is repeatability after NFL departures (Bailey, Rodriguez).
s: Reigning #1 defense with the resources to re-stock
Tempo + explosiveness stresses any defense's conditioning
Administration fully committed to paying for it
w: New QB in a system that needs one
2025 was aided by a friendly schedule draw — margins shrink
Can the portal-built model survive year-over-year turnover?

Georgia.ob: Mike Bobo pro-style — 12 personnel base, condensed formations, play-action keepers for Gunner Stockton's legs.
Georgia.ot: More QB-run element with Stockton than the Bennett/Beck eras. Explosiveness has been the missing gear two straight years; they manufacture chunk plays with tight-end seams rather than WR speed.
Georgia.db: Smart/Schumann multiple 3-4 — pattern-match zone, elite vs the run, linebacker-driven.
Georgia.dt: Still top-shelf on early downs, but 2025 showed uncharacteristic explosive leaks in the secondary. The front seven reloads annually; the back end is the retooling project.
s: Line-of-scrimmage depth on both sides remains SEC-best
Smart's floor: double-digit wins in every full season
Stockton's mobility adds a red-zone dimension
w: WR separation and vertical speed — two years running
Secondary explosives allowed trending the wrong way
Offense can go conservative in exactly the games that decide seasons

Utah.ob: Morgan Scalley finally takes over (HC-in-waiting since 2024) after Whittingham's exit to Michigan — Jason Beck's QB-run spread around Devon Dampier should carry over intact.
Utah.ot: Dampier returning keeps the identity: zone-read, counter bash, designed keepers. Scalley ran the defense for a decade, so offensive continuity is the plan, not a hope — but confirm Beck stayed before air.
Utah.db: Morgan Scalley's 4-2-5 — structurally disciplined, safeties downhill, almost never misaligned.
Utah.dt: Classic Utah: top-20 success-rate defense, low havoc variance, tackle everything. They'll trade completions for time-of-possession and red-zone stops.
s: Scalley is the most prepared first-year HC in the country
Dampier is the Big 12's most valuable returning QB

Ole Miss.ob: Pete Golding's first year as HC after Kiffin's LSU exit — but the spread-tempo offensive core persists, now built around a returning star.
Ole Miss.ot: Trinidad Chambliss is BACK for 2026 after winning his NCAA eligibility appeal — the reigning SEC Newcomer of the Year who quarterbacked a 13-win, first-ever CFP season (Sugar Bowl MVP in the win over Georgia; season ended in the Fiesta vs Miami). Ex-Auburn five-star Deuce Knight waits behind him.
Ole Miss.db: Golding's own 4-2-5 system stays — multiple fronts, sim pressures, transfer-heavy DL.
Ole Miss.dt: The identity Golding built as DC: disruptive interior line, aggressive early-down run fits, press man in must-pass. He kept the defensive spine through the transition.
s: Chambliss chose Oxford over following Kiffin — the rarest kind of continuity win
Program-record 13-win CFP season as the springboard
QB room is two-deep with high-end talent (Chambliss + Knight)
w: First-year head coach following the program's identity engine
Lost OC-level play-calling brains to Baton Rouge
The league now has a full offseason of Chambliss film

Alabama.ob: Ryan Grubb's dropback pass game — five-out spacing, chunk throws to the intermediate middle, RB screens as run-game extension.
Alabama.ot: Ty Simpson's draft departure hands it to Keelon Russell (five-star, year two). Pass-first identity is set; the run game (#102 rush EPA feel in stretches) and short-yardage remain the friction points.
Alabama.db: Kane Wommack's 4-2-5 'swarm' — light boxes, rally speed, disguised pressure.
Alabama.dt: Elite in coverage, occasionally soft vs downhill gap runs. Special teams (SP+ ST -1.30, #103) actively lost them field position all season — a fixable, glaring leak.
s: Russell is the most talented QB in the building since Bryce Young
WR room reloads with five-stars annually
Wommack's coverage disguise ranks with anyone's
w: Special teams was a bottom-35 unit — inexcusable at this level
First-year starter at QB in Tuscaloosa expectations

Texas.ob: Sarkisian pro-style — wide zone marriage with play-action dagger/flood, 12 personnel sprinkled, Arch Manning at the wheel.
Texas.ot: 2025 underdelivered on the preseason #1 hype: OL regression and drops, not Manning, were the story. Expect more designed Manning movement and a rebuilt interior line in 2026.
Texas.db: Pete Kwiatkowski's 4-2-5 — light-box quarters, elite eraser safeties, pressure with four.
Texas.dt: Back end is the program's best unit: top-10 pass efficiency defense three years running. Vulnerable stretch: physical duo/counter teams that stay on schedule.
s: Manning's arm talent + second full year as starter
PK's secondary erases one side of the field
Roster depth built for a 16-game season
w: OL interior was the 2025 disappointment — retooled, unproven
Run-game identity wobbles when zone gets stuffed early
Pressure to deliver now is at maximum

Texas A&M.ob: Collin Klein's spread — Marcel Reed's legs as a base constraint, quick RPO game, shot plays off sprint-out movement.
Texas A&M.ot: Reed returning after the 2025 CFP run makes this a top-10 offense on paper: designed QB run in the red zone, improving pocket patience. WR depth thinned by the draft.
Texas A&M.db: Mike Elko's multiple front — heavy nickel, twist-game interior pressure, coverage rotation late in the down.
Texas A&M.dt: Elko's fingerprints: top-25 success rate allowed, disciplined vs RPOs. Edge rush production must be re-sourced after draft losses.
s: Reed is the SEC's best returning dual-threat resume
Elko game-days: consistently steal one possession a game
Trench classes stacked three years deep
w: Receiver room needs a portal hit to stay explosive
Program still hasn't stacked two elite years in a row

USC.ob: Lincoln Riley's Air Raid tree — spacing and leverage masterclass, best screen game in the country, tempo when wounded.
USC.ot: Riley's system produces top-10 pass efficiency regardless of the trigger man; 2026 QB competition (post-Maiava room) decides the ceiling. Run game is opportunistic draw/counter, not identity.
USC.db: D'Anton Lynn's 4-2-5 — simulated pressure, split-safety shells, major 2025 improvement in structure.
USC.dt: From liability to league-average-plus in one Lynn year: fewer busts, better tackling. Still lean up front against B1G power run games.
s: Riley's pass-game design remains a per-play cheat code
Lynn defense trending up with his own recruits
w: Interior DL vs Michigan/Ohio State-style power remains the litmus test
QB transition year in a QB-dependent system
Cold-weather November road identity still unproven

Vanderbilt.ob: Tim Beck's option-flavored spread — but the Diego Pavia era is over, and the offense was built bespoke around his chaos.
Vanderbilt.ot: Two years of program-best offense came from QB-run keepers, fourth-down aggression, and Pavia's off-script genius. The 2026 offense must be re-founded: expect the same run-first, possession-length identity with a more conventional passer.
Vanderbilt.db: Clark Lea's 3-3-5 multiple — his own defensive rebuild caught up in 2025: opportunistic, turnover-hunting.
Vanderbilt.dt: They won 2025 games with third-down disguise and red-zone stinginess rather than raw talent. Regression risk is real as the league re-calibrates to them.
s: Lea has institutionalized an identity Vandy never had
Fourth-down/analytics edge is the sharpest in the SEC
Two straight bowl seasons of belief and retention
w: Replacing Pavia isn't a position change, it's an identity change
Talent gap vs the SEC's top half hasn't closed

Oklahoma.ob: Ben Arbuckle's Air-Raid-with-legs — John Mateer's dual-threat volume, empty formations with QB-draw constraint.
Oklahoma.ot: Mateer confirmed his return for a senior year ('I need to be better' — his words). 2025 was uneven: 62% completion, 14/11 TD-INT through a broken throwing hand he played through after 17 days. Healthy hand + year two in-system is the bull case; the designed-run gravity (8 rushing TDs) never left.
Oklahoma.db: Venables' 4-2-5 — cheat-the-count sim pressures, elite 2025 (top-5 EPA allowed), speed everywhere.
Oklahoma.dt: The best Venables defense yet: havoc without busts. Replacing interior anchors is the annual tax, but the scheme is now fully installed.
s: Mateer returning removes the biggest variable — chemistry with Arbuckle is year three
Venables defense hit elite in year four as promised
The Sooners finally look built for SEC line play
w: Mateer must cut the interceptions (11 in 2025) to justify the billing
WR explosiveness thin past the top two
OL depth one injury from trouble

Penn State.ob: Matt Campbell era begins (8-year deal from Iowa State, replacing the fired James Franklin) — expect his identity: multi-TE pro-style run game, play-action, culture-first program building.
Penn State.ot: Campbell turned Iowa State from 3-9 into a perennial bowl program with less talent than he inherits here. The roster skews power-spread with a top-15 OL two-deep and deep RB room; run-first ball control while the new staff installs, opening vs Marshall Sept 5.
Penn State.db: The defensive front is the program's export product — edge/DT depth stays elite through the transition. Note: 2025 DC Jim Knowles left for Tennessee, so the back end gets a new voice too.
Penn State.dt: 2025 was top-10 in success rate allowed before the season unraveled. Campbell's Iowa State defenses were famously structured (the 3-3-5 revolution started there) — expect scheme discipline over blitz volume.
s: Campbell is a proven program-builder with a top-10 roster for the first time
Trench talent on both sides survives regime change
w: QB position is a genuine unknown in year one
Coordinator turnover on both sides in one offseason
Campbell has never coached a season under this level of expectation

BYU.ob: Aaron Roderick's spread — Bear Bachmeier's true-freshman 2025 revelation, QB run married to deep play-action.
BYU.ot: Bachmeier returning for year two is the Big 12's quiet superpower: they punished single-high all season. Physical, patient, and better on 3rd-and-4-to-7 than anyone in the league.
BYU.db: Jay Hill's 3-3-5 — boundary pressure looks, disciplined eyes, turnover manufacturing.
BYU.dt: Top-20 havoc without gambling: Hill's blitzes hit home because the back end holds. Size vs SEC-style OLs is the only structural question.
s: Bachmeier is the best returning QB story in the Big 12
Hill's defense creates 2+ takeaways a game feel
Roster continuity in the portal era — nobody retains like BYU
w: Skill-position ceiling is modest by top-10 standards
Front sizes get tested by the league's biggest OLs
Thin margin for error with a top-heavy schedule

Iowa.ob: Tim Lester's outside zone — 12/21 personnel, the slowest pace in the sample (60.8 plays/g), play-action boots off it.
Iowa.ot: Gronowski's graduation reopens the eternal QB question. Elite line yards (#15) and points/opportunity (#20) say the infrastructure works; they shorten every game and bet on special teams.
Iowa.db: Phil Parker's quarters — two-high shells, drop-eight in must-pass, tackling machine.
Iowa.dt: #8 in explosiveness allowed: nothing over the top, ever. Low havoc by design; they'll concede 5-yard completions for sixty minutes and win the red zone.
s: Parker is the best pure DC in the sport, year in year out
Special teams wins them a game a season outright
Identity so stable it survives any roster
w: QB play caps the ceiling — again
Modern tempo offenses stress their substitution patterns

Tennessee.ob: Heupel's Veer and Shoot — extreme splits, vertical stems, tempo that forbids substitution.
Tennessee.ot: Post-Aguilar QB room is open; the system's floor is high (spacing does the work) but its ceiling is QB-dependent. Run game is inside zone vs light boxes the splits create.
Tennessee.db: New DC Jim Knowles (via Penn State, and the 2024 natty-winning Ohio State defense before that) replaces Tim Banks, who left for Auburn's staff after the unit collapsed to #92 in scoring defense.
Tennessee.dt: Knowles brings his safety-driven 4-2-5 — late rotation, creative sim pressures, and a track record of immediate top-10 turnarounds. The scheme upgrade is the single biggest coordinator win of the SEC offseason.
s: Knowles is as good as defensive hires get
Scheme guarantees explosive plays regardless of personnel
OL continuity best of the Heupel era
w: QB competition with no proven answer
2025 defensive personnel didn't suddenly get faster — year-one Knowles fixes structure, not speed
Wide-split offense struggles vs elite two-high discipline

Michigan.ob: New era: Kyle Whittingham (hired from Utah after Sherrone Moore's December firing) inherits Bryce Underwood's year two — expect a physical, QB-run-friendly structure around the #1 recruit's arm.
Michigan.ot: Whittingham's Utah offenses were identity-stable: gap runs, play-action, low turnovers. Pairing that floor with Underwood's ceiling is the bet; the OC hire and system blend are the July unknowns — verify the staff before air.
Michigan.db: Wink Martindale's pressure-man — highest blitz-rate profile in the conference, corners on islands.
Michigan.dt: Boom-bust by design: elite havoc, occasional explosive concessions when the pressure loses. They erase bad QBs entirely.
s: Underwood's ceiling is the B1G's highest
Whittingham brings two decades of program-toughness credibility
Championship-grade DL two-deep survives the change
w: HC transition on top of a year-two QB — variance stacks
Whittingham has never coached east of the Rockies
WR separation remains the thinnest asset

Washington.ob: Jedd Fisch pro-style spread — Demond Williams' dual-threat base, movement pockets, layered crossers.
Washington.ot: Williams quietly finished 2025 top-20 in EPA/play: the legs are real, and Fisch's route tree gives him easy first reads. OL rebuild is the throttle.
Washington.db: Multiple 4-2-5 — coverage-first, rally tackling, modest havoc.
Washington.dt: Competent, not scary: they limit explosives and force long drives, but generate little negative-play pressure without blitzing.
s: Williams is a rising top-10 QB nobody outside the West watches
w: Trench depth (both lines) below B1G contender grade
Pass rush needs a portal answer

Missouri.ob: Drinkwitz spread — Ahmad Hardy's downhill volume behind heavy pre-snap motion, Beau Pribula-era RPO game.
Missouri.ot: Hardy led the nation in rushing as a sophomore; everything constraints off him. Austin Simmons arrives from Ole Miss to settle the QB room — an SEC-experienced arm to keep defenses out of nine-man boxes.
Missouri.db: Corey Batoon's 4-2-5 — spot-drop discipline, interior push over edge speed.
Missouri.dt: Top-25 success rate allowed in 2025; the profile is stout-vs-run, adequate-vs-pass. They keep games in the 20s.
s: Hardy is the SEC's best returning RB — full stop
Home environment consistently banks an upset
w: QB clarity needed before the identity fully clicks
Ceiling historically caps at 10 wins — breaking through needs variance

SMU.ob: Rhett Lashlee tempo-RPO spread — wide splits, snap-count warfare, RB screens as extended runs.
SMU.ot: Kevin Jennings' era likely closes; the system has produced a top-25 offense three straight years with three different QBs. Expect the same: pace, spacing, and YAC manufacturing.
SMU.db: 3-3-5 pressure-sim front — transfer-heavy back seven, gambling profile.
SMU.dt: Havoc-rich, bust-prone: they'll win you three games with takeaways and lose you one with explosives. ACC offenses solved stretches of 2025.
s: System offense with proven QB plug-and-play
Dallas NIL base keeps the roster stocked
Lashlee's in-game tempo manipulation is elite
w: Defensive floor is the concern in big games
Line play still a tier below ACC's best
Post-CFP expectations meet a harder schedule

Auburn.ob: Alex Golesh's tempo, TE-friendly vertical spread arrives from USF — OC Joel Gordon came with him (his play-caller all three Tampa years; #2 national total offense in 2025).
Auburn.ot: Golesh offenses live in 12-personnel spacing at Veer-and-Shoot pace — the exact inverse of the Freeze stagnation. Staff assembled in 17 days: Kodi Burns (WRs, Auburn alum), Larry Porter (RBs), Tim Banks (safeties, from Tennessee). QB room portal-rebuilt (Deuce Knight left for Ole Miss).
Auburn.db: Continuity where it counts: DJ Durkin RETAINED as DC — the fronts, sim pressures, and personnel carry over intact.
Auburn.dt: Keeping Durkin means the defense skips the reinstall tax entirely. DL talent accumulation is top-10 nationally; the 2025 defense was better than its record.
s: Golesh's offense fixes the exact thing that got Freeze fired
Durkin retention = one-sided transition, not a teardown
Jordan-Hare in year-one-energy mode is worth a home upset
w: Full identity reinstall on offense — year-one variance is huge
QB is a total unknown after the Knight exit
SEC schedule shows no mercy to transitions

South Florida.ob: Brian Hartline era — Ohio State's OC/WR-whisperer took the job in December 2025. Expect a pro-spread with elite receiver development replacing Golesh's tempo-vertical system.
South Florida.ot: Byrum Brown's graduation plus the system change makes this a double reset, but Hartline is a Tampa-area legend recruiting his own backyard. Final season at Raymond James before the on-campus stadium opens in 2027 — momentum is real even in transition.
South Florida.db: The 2025 defense was aggressive, multiple, and turnover-hungry — how much survives the staff turnover is the open question.
South Florida.dt: USF punched above its weight through scheme and culture; the architect left, but the administration's investment (and Hartline's recruiting pull) suggests a reload, not a collapse. Grade the two-deep in September, not July.
s: Hartline is arguably the best WR developer in football, college or pro
Tampa recruiting footprint is genuinely fertile — and now has a closer
Administration showed it will invest after the CFP near-miss
w: First-time head coach replacing a proven one, with a new QB
System change on offense means year-one friction
FPI's #25 is a lagging indicator — treat with maximum skepticism

LSU.ob: Lane Kiffin's tempo-RPO spread arrives in Baton Rouge — the same pace-and-glance system that just produced Ole Miss's 13-win CFP season, now with LSU's talent base.
LSU.ot: QB room rebuilt through the portal after Garrett Nussmeier's draft exit (his stock fell behind a bad 2025 OL — the roster started the offseason with one scholarship arm). Kiffin installs fast; week 1 won't look like a rebuild even if November does.
LSU.db: Multiple 4-2-5 rebuild under Kiffin's hires — verify the coordinator before air; the transition consumed the winter.
LSU.dt: The 2025 defense underachieved amid the Brian Kelly firing chaos — talent has outrun results in Baton Rouge for two years. Kiffin's Ole Miss defenses were portal-patched and opportunistic; expect the same blueprint.
s: Kiffin's offense + LSU's recruiting base is the SEC's scariest combination on paper
NIL/portal muscle to patch any hole fast
w: Portal QB must hit immediately — no developmental runway
Mid-season firing chaos tax: two systems, two staffs in one year
OL was the 2025 failure point and returns unproven

South Carolina.ob: Shane Beamer's spread built around LaNorris Sellers — designed QB run, play-action verticals, the offense flowing entirely through #16.
South Carolina.ot: Sellers RETURNED — a projected top-5 NFL pick who chose Columbia over the draft, the single biggest returning-player win in the conference. 2025 wasted him behind poor protection; the whole offseason was spent fixing his supporting cast.
South Carolina.db: 4-2-5 with the edge-rush pipeline the program has become known for — speed off both edges, safeties downhill.
South Carolina.dt: Better than its 2025 record: the defense kept games winnable while the offense sputtered. Front-seven athleticism is SEC-caliber; depth behind it is not.
s: Sellers is arguably the SEC's best player at any position
Beamer's special teams steal hidden points
w: The OL must stop getting Sellers hit — same sentence as last July
Skill weapons a tier below the SEC's best
Staff pressure: wasting Sellers' return is a fireable offense

Florida.ob: Jon Sumrall (from Tulane) brings his physical, run-first spread — QB run game, heavy play-action, ball-control identity.
Florida.ot: DJ Lagway left for Baylor after the coaching change, so the QB is a portal answer running a brand-new system. Sumrall's Tulane teams won with discipline, turnover margin, and fourth-down conviction — the exact traits late-Napier Florida lacked.
Florida.db: Sumrall is a defense-and-culture head coach: aggressive 4-2-5, tackling-first, simplified assignments.
Florida.dt: The roster has more SEC talent than its 2025 results showed — misused, not missing. Expect immediate physicality gains and early-season simplicity.
s: Sumrall has the best culture track record of any recent mid-major riser
DL and secondary talent stockpiled from top-10 classes
Roster is battle-hardened by the league's toughest schedules
w: QB reset after Lagway's exit — new system, new triggerman
Second offensive system in two years for the returners
The Swamp does not grade on a transition curve

Mississippi State.ob: Jeff Lebby's year-three tempo spread (Veer-and-Shoot family) around rising QB KaMario Taylor — vertical shots off wide splits at pace.
Mississippi State.ot: Taylor is the league's quiet breakout pick, with Kevin Johns (from Oklahoma State) hired to coach the QB room. Scoring won't be the problem; it never is in Lebby's offense by year three.
Mississippi State.db: Rebuilt defensive staff for 2026 — the 2025 unit was among the SEC's worst on early downs.
Mississippi State.dt: They'll score and get scored on: the win total rides entirely on the defense climbing from bad to mediocre. Watch shootout unders... which is to say, overs.
s: Taylor's trajectory is real — dual-threat with a live arm
Lebby's offense travels regardless of talent gaps
Year-three continuity is rare currency in this league's chaos
w: Defense still bottom-tier SEC after two rebuild years
Two-deep depth thins fast at contact positions
Zero margin for error in this schedule

Kentucky.ob: Will Stein — Oregon's offensive coordinator — takes over after Kentucky moved on from Mark Stoops following back-to-back losing seasons. His motion-heavy wide-zone spread replaces a decade of grind-it-out identity.
Kentucky.ot: Stein was the hottest OC hire on the market; the roster he inherits was built for the opposite style. Expect a portal-heavy skill infusion and a year-one offense that looks modern immediately but runs thin by November.
Kentucky.db: The Stoops-era defensive identity leaves with him — new coordinator, new structure (verify the staff before air).
Kentucky.dt: Kentucky's floor was always its defense under Stoops; that floor is now unknown. The rebuild is total, both sides.
s: Stein's offense is the anti-Stoops — pace, space, and QB-friendly structure
Expectations at rock bottom: everything is upside
First-mover credibility with portal offensive skill
w: Full-program reset — scheme, staff, and roster simultaneously
Roster was recruited for a different sport, stylistically
The SEC schedule doesn't wait for installs

Arkansas.ob: Ryan Silverfield (50-25 at Memphis, two 10-win seasons) brings a balanced pro-spread with a strong OL-development reputation.
Arkansas.ot: The offense will be built around efficiency and the run game while the portal sorts the QB room. Silverfield's Memphis teams were clean: low turnovers, high red-zone conversion, few beat-yourself losses.
Arkansas.db: New staff, fundamentals-first rebuild — reports pointed at an in-state coordinator hire; verify before air.
Arkansas.dt: The 2025 defense was bottom-quartile SEC and the talent gap is real. Year one is about structure, not stars.
s: Silverfield develops OL and culture — the two things Arkansas lacked
A genuinely fresh start after the Pittman plateau
Soft on-ramp: opens with North Alabama Sept 5
w: Talent gap vs even the SEC's middle class

Illinois.ob: Bielema's pro-style spread — play-action heavy, TE-friendly, built on OL development.
Illinois.ot: Luke Altmyer's graduation opens the QB room after the winningest three-year run in program history. The formula stays: protect the ball, win the hidden yards, play from ahead.
Illinois.db: 4-2-5 two-high shell — takeaway-driven, disciplined, rarely blitz-reliant.
Illinois.dt: Consistently top-25 in turnover margin under Bielema; they concede yards between the 20s and clamp in the red zone.
w: Skill speed below B1G contender grade

Maryland.ob: Locksley's pass-first spread around Malik Washington — the true-freshman revelation of 2025 returns as the conference's most exciting young arm.
Maryland.ot: Washington threw it 40+ times a game down the stretch; expect a more balanced year two with a rebuilt OL in front of him.
Maryland.db: 4-2-5 with rebuilt back seven — 2025 collapsed late-season yet again.
Maryland.dt: The pattern is Maryland's curse: competitive Septembers, brutal Novembers. The defense, not Washington, decides if that changes.
s: Washington is a future star — genuine top-15-pick trajectory
w: Defense a tier below the offense's arc

Michigan State.ob: Pat Fitzgerald returns to coaching in East Lansing after Jonathan Smith's firing — expect his identity: physical inside run, play-action, field position.
Michigan State.ot: Fitzgerald's Northwestern teams won with structure and special teams, never talent. MSU gives him more raw material than Evanston ever did; the QB room is unsettled.
Michigan State.db: 4-3-quarters roots — sound, physical, turnover-hunting. Staff assembly ongoing; verify coordinators before air.
Michigan State.dt: The floor rises immediately (Fitzgerald teams don't beat themselves); the ceiling depends on portal talent acquisition catching up.
s: Fresh energy after two failed rebuilds
B1G-caliber facilities and NIL waiting to be used
w: Roster talent bottom-third of the B1G
Fitzgerald's last CFB game was 2022 — rust is real

Minnesota.ob: Fleck's run-first, boat-rowing ball control — outside zone, max-protect shots, Drake Lindsey managing.
Minnesota.ot: Lindsey's sophomore year showed enough to keep the job; the identity never changes: shorten the game, win 4th quarters with the OL.
Minnesota.db: 4-2-5 under Corey Hetherman's former structure — disciplined, low-explosives.
Minnesota.dt: Perpetually top-30 in explosive-play prevention; pressure comes from scheme. They lose to teams that out-talent them, never to chaos.
s: The most stable identity in the West division footprint
Elite explosive-play prevention year over year
Fleck wins the games he should
w: Loses the line of scrimmage vs elite fronts

Nebraska.ob: Rhule's pro-spread — quick game and RPOs with Dylan Raiola, TJ Lateef pushing after his freshman flashes during Raiola's injury.
Nebraska.ot: Raiola's return from the broken leg is the season's hinge; Lateef's late-2025 play created a genuine competition. Skill room rebuilt through the portal again.
Nebraska.db: 3-3-5 pressure system — Rhule's rebuilt front seven finally has B1G bodies.
Nebraska.dt: Year-three Rhule defenses historically spike; 2025's close-game curse (one-score losses again) was offense-inflicted.
s: Rhule's year-three track record (Temple, Baylor both spiked)
QB room has two legitimate answers
w: Still hasn't beaten a top-15 team under Rhule

Northwestern.ob: Braun-ball: outside zone, heavy formations, field-position chess with a new QB after Preston Stone's one-year rental.
Northwestern.ot: The new lakeside stadium opens with the same formula: shorten games, live in third-and-manageable, upset one ranked team a year.
Northwestern.db: 4-3 quarters, fundamentals-first — routinely better than its talent.
Northwestern.dt: Braun's defenses tackle and align; they lose to speed, not scheme. Explosives allowed spike only against top-15 offenses.
w: Zero explosive offense two years running

Purdue.ob: Barry Odom's year two — spread with QB run elements, still hunting an identity after a bottom-of-the-league 2025.
Purdue.ot: The portal churned the entire two-deep again. Whatever emerges, the schedule (B1G West-legacy draws) offers a winnable September.
Purdue.db: Odom's 3-3-5 pressure roots — the one unit that showed year-one progress.
Purdue.dt: Havoc rates climbed late in 2025; the back end remains the league's most targetable.
s: Odom rebuilt UNLV fast — the blueprint exists
Low bar: any bowl is a win
w: Talent gap vs the league median is wide

Rutgers.ob: Schiano's pro-style — power run, play-action, new QB after Kaliakmanis' three-year run ended.
Rutgers.ot: The Rutgers formula is fixed: win the kicking game, run it 55%, keep games under 50 snaps of stress. Athan's departure makes the pass game a projection.
Rutgers.db: 4-3 base with Schiano's DB pedigree — sound, physical, rarely shredded twice the same way.
Rutgers.dt: Quietly top-40 defense most years; the offense's inability to stretch fields keeps it on the field too long.
w: Pass offense among the B1G's worst
New QB with a thin WR room
Ceiling is bowl eligibility, not the CFP race

UCLA.ob: Bob Chesney arrives from James Madison after the DeShaun Foster experiment failed — his JMU teams were fast-tempo, RPO-heavy overachievers.
UCLA.ot: Nico Iamaleava's status defined the offseason (draft-eligible after a turbulent two years) — verify the QB room before air. Chesney's system elevates mid-tier talent; Westwood gives him more.
UCLA.db: Chesney brings the JMU pressure identity — multiple fronts, havoc-hunting.
UCLA.dt: The 2025 defense quit late; a full culture rebuild is underway. G5-to-P4 coaching jumps hit defense first — expect year-one turbulence.
s: Chesney has won everywhere he's been — three straight successful builds
LA talent base underexploited for a decade
Tempo identity fits the roster's athletes
w: G5-to-B1G jump is the biggest in coaching
Program has churned three HCs in five years

Wisconsin.ob: Fickell's survival pivot: run-first 12 personnel behind the league's most veteran OL — the Air Raid detour is dead.
Wisconsin.ot: After three years of identity crisis, 2025's late-season run-heavy shift saved the program's dignity and Fickell's job. The QB is a game-manager by design now.
Wisconsin.db: Fickell's 3-3-5 remains legitimately good — the offense wasted it two years running.
Wisconsin.dt: Top-25 defensive metrics in back-to-back seasons with zero offensive support. If the run identity holds, the wins math flips.
s: Defense has been quietly elite through the chaos
OL continuity best in the West footprint
w: Skill talent drained by three portal cycles

Arizona.ob: Brent Brennan's spread resets around a new QB after Noah Fifita's four-year era ended.
Arizona.ot: 2025's rebound (bowl return) came from defense and a softer schedule; the offense turns over its top three passers and its identity with them.
Arizona.db: 4-2-5 with legitimate back-seven talent — the roster's strength.
Arizona.dt: Top-40 pass defense in 2025; the front six needs a portal infusion to hold the line vs the league's power runs.

Arizona State.ob: Dillingham's tempo-spread — Sam Leavitt's return from injury restores the league's best QB-coach chemistry outside Lubbock.
Arizona State.ot: When healthy, Leavitt runs the full menu: RPOs, movement pockets, designed keepers. The 2024 CFP run's supporting cast is mostly gone; 2025 proved the system survives churn.
Arizona State.dt: Havoc-rich and bust-prone; they need the offense scoring 30+ to let the gambles ride.
s: Leavitt-Dillingham is a top-10 QB-HC pairing
Tempo stresses the league's thin two-deeps

Baylor.ob: Aranda survived and won the offseason: DJ Lagway arrives from Florida — the highest-rated transfer in program history running Jake Spavital's spread.
Baylor.ot: Sawyer Robertson's graduation plus Lagway's arrival makes this the league's most fascinating offense: elite arm talent meeting a proven system. Waco expectations just tripled.
Baylor.db: Aranda's multiple front — his personal play-calling stint continues; disguise-heavy, light boxes.
Baylor.dt: 2025 defense was middling; Aranda's own scheme brain remains the unit's best asset and its bottleneck (HC doing DC things).
s: Lagway is a program-changing talent get
Aranda's defensive mind with his seat finally cool
w: Aranda's game-management still costs a game a year

Cincinnati.ob: Satterfield's balanced spread — Brendan Sorsby's breakout 2025 made this a top-25 offense; his draft decision defined the offseason (verify the QB room).
Cincinnati.ot: If Sorsby's back, everything works: QB run, play-action seams, the league's most underrated OL. If not, it's a reset.
Cincinnati.db: 3-3-5 — improved but still the program's limiting factor.
Cincinnati.dt: Bottom-half in havoc and explosives allowed; they won 2025 shootouts by having the better QB.
s: Sorsby (if returning) is top-3 in the league

Colorado.ob: Prime's year four — Julian Lewis' offense now, with the five-star sophomore taking full ownership after splitting 2025.
Colorado.ot: The Shedeur/Hunter era's afterglow faded fast in 2025; Lewis is the reboot. Expect pro-style dropback with Shurmur-tree structure and a rebuilt OL — again.
Colorado.db: 4-2-5 — Livingston-era aggression with annual portal turnover.
Colorado.dt: Boulder's defense has been a new cast every September; talent flashes, cohesion lags. Year-four culture verdict comes due.
s: Prime's brand still wins portal battles
w: OL remains the program's chronic failure
Defense unproven for the third straight year

Houston.ob: Willie Fritz ball: run-first, clock-first, QB-run spread — the 2025 bowl return validated the rebuild's floor.
Houston.ot: The QB room remains committee-flavored; Fritz teams historically peak in year three, which is now.
Houston.db: 4-2-5, sound and physical — the roster's better half.
Houston.dt: Top-half Big 12 defense in 2025 on effort and structure; talent acquisition hasn't caught up to scheme yet.

Iowa State.ob: Jimmy Rogers (via Washington State, before that 15-0 at South Dakota State) replaces Campbell — expect his physical, TE-heavy, defense-first program model.
Iowa State.ot: Rocco Becht's graduation plus the HC change makes the offense a full reset; Rogers' FCS-dynasty pedigree says structure arrives before stars.
Iowa State.db: Rogers is a defensive builder — the Cyclone 3-3-3 tradition should evolve, not vanish.
Iowa State.dt: Campbell left the cupboard solid but the stars left with him (transfers followed him to PSU). Grade this roster in October.
s: Rogers won everywhere, including a natty run at SDSU
Big 12 middle is soft enough to stay bowl-bound
w: Campbell took momentum and some players east
First P4 HC job — the jump is real

Kansas.ob: Leipold's post-Daniels era begins — the veteran spread stays, the six-year QB finally graduated.
Kansas.ot: Leipold's system has produced top-40 offenses with three different QBs; the OL and TE room make the floor. The new starter inherits structure, not chaos.
Kansas.dt: The defense keeps Kansas in games it has no business winning; explosives allowed spike vs elite speed.
s: Leipold is the league's best pure program coach
w: Talent ceiling honest: 8 wins is the peak

Kansas State.ob: Klieman's spread-option hybrid around Avery Johnson's final act — the league's most schemed QB-run offense.
Kansas State.ot: Johnson's decision to return (verify) sets the ceiling; the RB-by-committee and elite special teams do the rest. 2025 underachieved the talent.
Kansas State.db: 4-3 quarters, disciplined — the Wildcat formula since Snyder.
Kansas State.dt: Rarely elite, never bad: 15 straight years of top-60 defense. Wins come from +1.5 turnover margin game scripts.
s: Best special teams in the league
Klieman never has a lost season
w: Thin margins vs the league's new money teams

Oklahoma State.ob: Eric Morris (from North Texas, Air-Raid tree) inherits the post-Gundy rebuild — tempo and spacing arrive in Stillwater.
Oklahoma State.ot: The 2025 season was a teardown after Gundy's September firing; Morris built a top-10 G5 offense at UNT and brings the blueprint north. QB comes from the portal.
Oklahoma State.db: Full defensive rebuild — 2025 was the league's worst unit by most measures.
Oklahoma State.dt: There is nowhere to go but up: bottom-10 nationally in EPA allowed. Morris' UNT defenses were opportunistic, not stout.
s: Morris' offense scores immediately everywhere he's been
OSU's facilities/NIL better than its 2025 record
w: Worst P4 roster inherited this cycle
The FPI residual (-12.3) was real, not noise

TCU.ob: Dykes' Air Raid descendant — Josh Hoover's 3,900-yard 2025 makes his draft decision the program's fulcrum (verify).
TCU.ot: With Hoover: top-15 passing offense, four-wide spacing, tempo bursts. Without: Dykes has recruited well enough to reload, not repeat.
TCU.db: 3-3-5 — improved to league-average after two leaky years.
TCU.dt: Gillespie's unit finally stopped the bleeding in 2025; run defense on standard downs remains the soft spot.
s: Hoover (if back) is the league's best pure passer
Dykes wins the ones he should
w: Fort Worth NIL trails the league's arms race

UCF.ob: Scott Frost's year two — tempo-option spread, the 2017 nostalgia hire now needing 2026 results.
UCF.ot: Year one was ugly (bottom-half Big 12); the system needs a dual-threat trigger the portal was tasked to find. Tempo without efficiency is just fast punting.
UCF.dt: Orlando talent keeps the athletes flowing; structure and two-deep depth lag. Explosives allowed were a league-worst issue.
s: Frost's system has a proven UCF ceiling
w: Year one showed no offensive identity

West Virginia.ob: Rich Rodriguez year two — the QB-run spread's originator still installing his triple-threat backfield vision.
West Virginia.ot: 2025 was a 6-win transition with flashes; the portal brought his kind of QB (verify the winner). Pace + QB run + vertical RPOs is the endgame.
West Virginia.db: 3-3-5 stack (Rodriguez tradition) — pressure from depth, gambles behind.
West Virginia.dt: Casteel-tree structure returning; 2025 was leaky vs power runs. Morgantown defenses round into form in odd years, history says.
s: Rodriguez's system fits the roster he's building

Boston College.ob: Bill O'Brien's pro-style — TE-centric, protection-first, NFL install running on a college clock.
Boston College.ot: The QB room stays unsettled after two years of rotation; O'Brien's system rewards whoever protects the ball. Run game leans gap-scheme behind a veteran interior.
Boston College.dt: They align right and tackle; havoc is bottom-quartile ACC and pass rush is a committee. Games stay close on structure alone.

California.ob: Tosh Lupoi (from Oregon DC) takes over post-Wilcox, keeping sophomore Jaron-Keawe Sagapolutele — the ACC's best young lefty — as the centerpiece.
California.ot: Sagapolutele's freshman 3,000-yard flash is the program's asset; expect a modern spread built around his rhythm game (OC hire defines the details — verify).
California.db: Lupoi's specialty: movement fronts, deep DL rotation, recruiting-driven talent upgrades.
California.dt: The Wilcox-era discipline stays in the walls; Lupoi's Oregon fronts were faster than anything Cal fielded. Transition friction is the only question.
s: Sagapolutele is a two-year franchise QB
Lupoi recruits at a level Cal hasn't seen
w: Skill room needs two portal hits

Clemson.ob: Dabo's pro-spread — 2025 was the program's worst season in 15 years, and the offense resets around a new QB after Klubnik.
Clemson.ot: Garrett Riley's system needs a distributor; the freshman class has the pedigree. The bigger question is philosophical: Dabo finally used the portal — barely.
Clemson.db: Tom Allen's aggressive 4-2-5 — the one 2025 unit that kept its standard.
Clemson.dt: Front-seven talent is still blue-chip; the 2025 collapse was offense and vibes. Explosive allowance stayed top-25.
s: Blue-chip roster ratio still top-5 nationally
A wounded Dabo has responded before
w: Portal resistance is now a proven handicap
Program aura of inevitability is gone

Duke.ob: Manny Diaz's reigning ACC CHAMPIONS — Darian Mensah's title-winning offense now needs a new arm after his Miami transfer.
Duke.ot: The system (tempo spread, layered play-action) returns intact with the OC; the QB who ran it doesn't. Duke's roster is the deepest it's been in program history.
Duke.db: Diaz's pressure-forward 4-2-5 — top-20 havoc, turnover manufacturing.
Duke.dt: The championship formula was defense + Mensah; the defense half returns. They'll blitz on 3rd-and-4 with a lead in the fourth. Fear nothing.
s: Reigning ACC champs with the DC-turned-HC formula working
Program money and momentum at all-time highs
w: Title defense with a target on every week

Florida State.ob: Norvell's tempo-spread survived the 2024 disaster with a 2025 rebound — year seven runs through a veteran portal QB room.
Florida State.ot: Malzahn-influenced run game (yes, that hire) with Norvell's RPO structure; identity is more physical than the CFP-era teams.
Florida State.db: Multiple 4-2-5 rebuilt via portal for the third time.
Florida State.dt: 2025 was league-average with flashes; the talent is sufficient, cohesion decides. Tallahassee's boom-bust cycle continues.
w: Program whiplash: CFP to 2-10 to reset in 36 months

Georgia Tech.ob: Key's option-flavored spread lost Haynes King but kept the identity: QB run game, heavy motion, fourth-down conviction.
Georgia Tech.ot: King's toughness defined the 10-win 2025 near-CFP season. The new QB inherits the league's best-schemed run game and its most creative situational calls.
Georgia Tech.db: 3-3-5 — improved yearly under Key, still the lesser half.
Georgia Tech.dt: They stay in structure and force long drives; elite speed still stresses the perimeter. Progress is real but incremental.
s: Key's schematic identity is the ACC's most distinct
w: Defense a year behind the offense

Louisville.ob: Brohm's QB-whisperer spread — a new arm (post-Miller Moss) in the most pass-friendly structure in the league.
Louisville.ot: Brohm's offenses finish top-25 regardless of the trigger man; the vertical timing game and TE seams stay. RB room is the sneaky strength.
Louisville.dt: Top-20 havoc, bottom-40 explosives allowed: the Brohm-era tradeoff in one line. They'll win a shootout and lose a coverage bust.
w: Coverage busts at the worst times

NC State.ob: Doeren's spread behind CJ Bailey's year three — the ACC's most experienced returning QB nobody talks about.
NC State.ot: Bailey's steady rise (14:4 ratio in 2025's back half) plus a veteran OL makes this the quiet dark horse. Identity: efficiency over explosion.
NC State.db: 3-3-3 hybrid — Doeren's defensive roots keep the floor.
NC State.dt: Middle-of-the-pack everything in 2025 with youth everywhere; the two-deep returns more production than any ACC defense.
s: Bailey is a top-5 returning ACC QB
Most returning production in the league
w: Special teams cost them two 2025 games

North Carolina.ob: Belichick year two — the NFL operating system (12 personnel, situational mastery) with a roster now fully his.
North Carolina.ot: Year one was a soap opera with a losing record; the football itself improved late. The QB room went full portal; the system asks for competence, not stardom.
North Carolina.dt: The defense tackled and aligned better than any recent UNC unit; it just couldn't rush P4 passers. Year two roster is bigger everywhere.
s: Late-2025 tape showed real structural progress
w: FPI's -12.1 residual said the market undersold the mess

Pittsburgh.ob: Narduzzi's pro-spread around Eli Holstein's third year — the QB flashes have never yet sustained.
Pittsburgh.ot: Holstein healthy = top-30 offense in stretches; the OL and drops betrayed 2025. Kade Bell's tempo influences stay.
Pittsburgh.db: Narduzzi's 4-3 press-quarters — the longest-running defensive identity in the ACC.
Pittsburgh.dt: Still top-30 in havoc, still gambling on corners' islands, still occasionally torched deep. You know exactly what this is.
w: The ceiling has been 8-4 for a decade

Stanford.ob: Tavita Pritchard returns to the Farm as HC (via the NFL) after the GM-era interim years — pro-style, QB-development-first.
Stanford.ot: Andrew Luck's GM structure now has its permanent coach. The roster is years from ACC-competitive; the identity (smart, physical, patient) is at least clear now.
Stanford.dt: Bottom-quartile ACC in most metrics; the two-deep is the league's thinnest. Progress = competitive Novembers, not bowls yet.
s: Luck's GM model brings NFL rigor
w: Multi-year rebuild, year one of it

Syracuse.ob: Fran Brown's physical spread — the QB room resets again after the Angeli year, with the run game now the identity.
Syracuse.ot: 2025 regressed hard once injuries hit; Brown's recruiting (top-25 classes) hasn't matured yet. Expect run-first ball control while it does.
Syracuse.db: 4-2-5 with Brown's DB-guru fingerprints — young and fast.
Syracuse.dt: The -12.1 FPI residual flagged them as market-overrated entering 2025, and the season proved it. The talent influx is real; 2026 is its first test.

Virginia.ob: Elliott's balanced spread coming off the 10-win breakthrough — but Chandler Morris' graduation empties the trigger seat.
Virginia.ot: The 2025 formula (veteran portal QB + turnover margin) worked once; replicating it is the test. OL returns four.
Virginia.db: 4-3 multiple — opportunistic, sound, senior-laden in 2025 and younger now.
Virginia.dt: The turnover luck (+14) will regress; the structure underneath was legitimately improved. Expect honest mid-tier.
w: Morris' void + turnover-luck regression = double drag

Virginia Tech.ob: James Franklin in Blacksburg — the shock hire of the cycle brings his pro-style program machine to a fanbase starving for relevance.
Virginia Tech.ot: Franklin's floor (24 winning seasons of infrastructure discipline) meets a roster Pry left thin. Expect a QB-run-friendly pro-spread and instant recruiting jumps.
Virginia Tech.dt: The 2025 unit was bottom-half ACC; Franklin hires proven coordinators and the lines improve first, historically.
s: The ACC's middle is very winnable

Wake Forest.ob: Jake Dickert's year two — the slow-mesh RPO tradition is gone; his identity is tempo-run and quarters defense.
Wake Forest.ot: Year one overachieved to bowl eligibility on discipline; the offense remains the ACC's least explosive by design and necessity both.
Wake Forest.db: 4-2-5 quarters — Dickert's specialty and the roster's better half.
Wake Forest.dt: Top-half ACC in success-rate defense immediately; the line of scrimmage still loses to the league's upper third.
w: Least explosive offense in the league

Oregon State.ob: JaMarcus Shephard (via Alabama co-OC) takes over the rebuilt-Pac-12 flagship — spread with WR-room pedigree.
Oregon State.ot: The two-year independence wilderness ends with a real conference; the roster churned annually and the QB room is open. Shephard's energy is the program bet.
Oregon State.db: 4-2-5 reset — Beaver defenses stayed respectable through the chaos.
Oregon State.dt: Corvallis keeps producing NFL DBs; the front needs restocking. The new Pac-12 schedule is winnable immediately.

Washington State.ob: Kirby Moore (from Missouri OC) inherits the Rogers-to-Ames fallout — his Drinkwitz-tree spread meets a roster on its third HC in three years.
Washington State.ot: Moore's Missouri offenses were motion-heavy and RB-led; Wazzu's roster fits the skill spots better than the lines. Rebuilt Pac-12 slate offers a fair fight.
Washington State.db: 3-3-5 tradition continues — speed over size, pressure over patience.
Washington State.dt: The two-deep survived the poaching better than 2024's version; structure will beat talent in this league's middle.
s: The new Pac-12 is winnable year one
w: Third HC in three years — churn tax

Army.ob: Monken's flexbone triple option — the purest run identity in FBS.
Army.ot: Post-Daily QB transition; the machine reloads inside. Clock control caps possessions near 8 a game.
Army.db: 3-4 bend-don't-break sized for option-week prep opponents.
Army.dt: Assignment-sound, undersized; they win by keeping your offense in street clothes.
w: Talent gaps show vs P4 speed

Charlotte.ob: Tim Albin's veer-and-shoot influence — rebuilt after a bleak-everything 2025.
Charlotte.ot: Year two of the Albin rebuild; QB from the portal. The offense showed late-season signs; the roster is still bottom-quartile American.
Charlotte.dt: Bottom-tier havoc and explosives allowed; progress is measured in competitive quarters.

East Carolina.ob: Houston's balanced spread — steady, veteran-driven, quietly 8-win capable.
East Carolina.ot: Katz-era QB stability ended; the run game and OL return the league's sneakiest floor.
East Carolina.dt: Sound vs the run, targetable deep; they keep games in the 20s.
s: Home crowd best in the league

Florida Atlantic.ob: Zach Kittley's Air Raid — the league's pace-and-volume passing lab.
Florida Atlantic.ot: Year two: the system produced yards immediately; points and wins need a defense. QB competition is open but the plug-in history is proven.
Florida Atlantic.dt: If it climbs to merely bad, the offense makes them a bowl team.

Memphis.ob: Charles Huff (from Southern Miss' turnaround) inherits a perennial 10-win machine.
Memphis.ot: Silverfield left a stocked roster; Huff's Marshall/USM teams ran harder than Memphis' recent identity — expect balance shift.
Memphis.dt: League-average unit with above-average takeaway timing; good enough behind that offense.

Navy.ob: Newberry's flexbone-with-RPO evolution — more pass than Army, same soul.
Navy.ot: Horvath's graduation hands it to the next trained pilot; the 2025 offense was top-20 rushing efficiency.
Navy.dt: The Army-Navy formula: erase explosives, force patience your opponent doesn't have.
s: Option efficiency with a real RPO layer
w: QB transition in a specialist system

North Texas.ob: Neal Brown returns to the G5 — inheriting the tempo machine Eric Morris built and took to Stillwater.
North Texas.ot: Drew Mestemaker's freshman breakout (led the 10-win run) returns; Brown's WVU pragmatism meets a ready-made spread. Verify Mestemaker stayed post-transition.
North Texas.db: 3-3-5 — the 2025 weak half of a great team.
North Texas.dt: Bottom-third league defense rode shotgun on a top-10 G5 offense; Brown's rebuild focus starts here.
s: Mestemaker is the league's best young QB

Rice.ob: Scott Abell's option-spread — the Davidson architect's second FBS year.
Rice.ot: 2025 overachieved to bowl eligibility on scheme surprise; year two loses the novelty edge.
Rice.dt: League-bottom talent playing league-median defense — coaching margin personified.

Temple.ob: K.C. Keeler's veteran program-building — year two off a surprise bowl.
Temple.ot: The FCS-legend playbook: transfers with chips, run-first pragmatism, special teams wins.
Temple.db: 4-2-5 — improved a full tier in one Keeler year.
Temple.dt: Structure over stars; November fades when depth runs out.

Tulane.ob: Will Hall promoted to keep the machine running after Sumrall's Florida exit.
Tulane.ot: The roster made back-to-back league title games; Hall's own USM offenses were RPO-heavy and RB-led. QB room resets again — it always does, and it always reloads.
Tulane.db: 4-2-5 — the league's most talented defense two years running.
Tulane.dt: Sumrall's structure remains in the walls; retention through the transition looked strong.

Tulsa.ob: Tre Lamb's year two — tempo spread with QB run, rebuilding from the league's basement.
Tulsa.ot: Year one showed fight and little else; the portal brought a new QB room.
Tulsa.dt: Bottom-quartile most metrics; progress is competitive Novembers.

UAB.ob: Alex Mortensen (Saban-tree, promoted from interim) — pass-first spread revival.
UAB.ot: The interim stretch showed a real offensive identity; full offseason install plus Birmingham's portal appeal is the bet.
UAB.dt: Nowhere to go but up from bottom-five nationally; even median defense means bowls.

UTSA.ob: Traylor's physical spread — RPO glance plus the hardest-playing culture in the league.
UTSA.ot: Post-Owens QB retool; the identity (third-down conversion machine, TE-friendly) is coach-proof by now.
UTSA.db: 4-2-5 veteran structure — always sound, occasionally special.
UTSA.dt: Top-half league unit annually; loses only the talent battles.
s: Traylor's culture is G5 gold standard
w: P4 poaching bleeds the two-deep yearly

Delaware.ob: Carty's balanced pro-spread — year two of FBS transition went bowl-eligible; the climb is real.
Delaware.ot: QB stability carried 2025; the jump to full CUSA rounds meets a thin two-deep.
Delaware.dt: Structure travels; line-of-scrimmage depth doesn't yet.
s: Culture from the FCS title runs

Florida International.ob: Willie Simmons' spread — the FAMU winner's first FBS build, year two.
Florida International.ot: Miami-area portal finds keep the skill room competitive; the lines lag a class behind.
Florida International.dt: Speed shows in flashes; sustaining four quarters is the project.
s: Simmons won big at every stop

Jacksonville State.ob: Run-heavy spread — the post-Rodriguez identity carried by a strong OL room.
Jacksonville State.ot: Back-to-back title-game trips prove the roster model; QB is a portal rental annually. Verify the current staff before air.
Jacksonville State.dt: Top-third league havoc; deep balls hurt them when the gambles miss.

Kennesaw State.ob: Bohannon's option-influenced ground game — the +16.4 FPI residual outlier of 2025.
Kennesaw State.ot: The market undersold them all season (our model flagged it); the triple-option DNA now blends portal spread elements.
Kennesaw State.dt: They tackle like an FCS-champion program because they were one.
s: Proved the doubters wrong once already

Liberty.ob: Chadwell's option-spread — QB-run identity hunting a return to the NY6 level.
Liberty.ot: 2025 disappointed (-7.9 FPI residual said the market overrated them); the QB room and OL retool.
Liberty.dt: League-average with big-play leaks; the offense must carry again.

Louisiana Tech.ob: Cumbie's Air Raid roots — pass-volume identity with a defense that finally showed up in 2025.
Louisiana Tech.ot: Bowl return built on the league's surprise defense; the offense now must match it.
Louisiana Tech.dt: Top-quartile league unit; regression risk with graduations.

Middle Tennessee.ob: Derek Mason's physical rebuild — defense-first culture in year two.
Middle Tennessee.ot: The offense remains the project; run-game commitment rose all 2025.
Middle Tennessee.dt: Improved a tier in year one; talent still bottom-half league.

Missouri State.ob: Casey Woods (from SMU OC) — FBS year two with a tempo-balanced attack.
Missouri State.ot: Transition roster overachieved in 2025; Woods brings P4 structure to the install.
Missouri State.dt: League-bottom talent, league-median effort; the gap closes slowly.

New Mexico State.ob: Tony Sanchez's veteran-laden spread — perennial scrappy underdog.
New Mexico State.ot: QB churn again; the identity is physical run and special teams.
New Mexico State.dt: Competitive floor established; ceilings require upsets.

Sam Houston.ob: Phil Longo's Air Raid — pass-first volume meets a program built on defense.
Sam Houston.ot: The -16.4 FPI residual (most underrated by market in our 2025 model) reflected a roster better than its brand. Longo's install year two should score.
Sam Houston.dt: Top-half league takeaways; size is the limiter.

UTEP.ob: Scotty Walden's tempo spread — the youngest HC energy in the league, year two.
UTEP.ot: Offense jumped a tier in 2025; the El Paso portal sell is culture and playing time.
UTEP.dt: Bottom-third league; effort present, depth absent.

Western Kentucky.ob: Helton-tree Air Raid tradition — pass-volume identity with annual portal QBs.
Western Kentucky.ot: The formula never changes: 300 yards passing, bowl eligibility, coordinator poaching. Verify HC continuity before air.
w: Ceiling capped at league title game

Akron.ob: Moorhead's RPO spread — year four of the slow climb.
Akron.ot: Offense finally showed his Penn State-era fingerprints in stretches.
Akron.dt: Bottom-third MAC; close-game record decides bowl math.

Ball State.ob: Mike Uremovich's power-spread rebuild, year two.
Ball State.ot: Ground-first identity taking hold; QB unsettled.
Ball State.dt: Improvement from awful to below-average — trajectory matters.

Bowling Green.ob: Eddie George's pro-style — the celebrity hire proved substance in year one.
Bowling Green.ot: Run-first, physical, TE-heavy; the locker room buys in fully.
Bowling Green.dt: League-median with a real identity; that's progress.

Buffalo.ob: Pete Lembo's special-teams-and-structure program — quietly excellent 2025.
Buffalo.ot: Efficient run game, low turnovers, field position mastery.
Buffalo.dt: Top-third MAC; they beat you with fewer mistakes.

Central Michigan.ob: Matt Drinkall's smashmouth build — Army-tree toughness, year two.
Central Michigan.ot: Run-heavy, huddle-up, clock-control; the MAC's most deliberate offense.
Central Michigan.dt: Improved on effort; personnel still catching up.

Eastern Michigan.ob: Creighton's veteran program — perennial 6-win bowl machine.
Eastern Michigan.ot: Portal QB again; the formula is competence plus timing.
Eastern Michigan.dt: Median MAC; turnover margin is the whole story.

Kent State.ob: Mark Carney (promoted) inherits the league's longest rebuild.
Kent State.ot: Roster still recovering from the Burns-era teardown; wins are found money.
Kent State.dt: Bottom of FBS metrics two years running.

Massachusetts.ob: Joe Harasymiak's return to MAC play — defense-first rebuild, year two.
Massachusetts.ot: The -11.3 FPI residual said the market overrated even the low bar; Minutemen football remains a grind.
Massachusetts.dt: The defense is honest; the offense is the abyss.

Miami (OH).ob: Chuck Martin's veteran pro-spread — the MAC's steadiest program.
Miami (OH).ot: Post-Gabbert QB transition; OL and TE play carry the identity.
Miami (OH).dt: Structure and tackling; they lose only to speed.
w: Ceiling is the MAC title game

Northern Illinois.ob: Hammock's power-run identity — MW-bound program playing its last MAC-style year? (verify alignment).
Northern Illinois.ot: RB-led, physical, low-variance; the 2024 Notre Dame upset aura faded into a rebuilding 2025.

Ohio.ob: John Hauser promoted after Brian Smith's single season — continuity for the MAC's best recent program.
Ohio.ot: The 2025 roster won 8+ again; Hauser's DC lens keeps the complementary-football identity.
Ohio.db: 4-2-5 — was the MAC's best unit under his coordination.
Ohio.dt: Top-quartile MAC defense returns its architect as HC.

Toledo.ob: Mike Jacobs (from D-II power Mercer... verify origin) inherits the MAC's most talented roster post-Candle.
Toledo.ot: Toledo out-talents the league annually; the transition question is retention, not scheme.
Toledo.dt: Best personnel in the MAC when motivated; consistency is the historic gap.
w: Candle took the standard with him

Western Michigan.ob: Lance Taylor's balanced pro-spread — steady build, year four.
Western Michigan.ot: Run-game identity with a veteran OL; bowl-level baseline established.
Western Michigan.dt: Median MAC; special teams above average.

Air Force.ob: Calhoun's flexbone — four decades of option devotion.
Air Force.ot: The cycle repeats: new QB, same machine, 300 rushing yards.
Air Force.dt: Erase explosives, steal possessions; the formula is eternal.

Boise State.ob: Danielson's pro-spread — post-Jeanty era found its footing in 2025.
Boise State.ot: Madsen's graduation opens a heated QB room; the RB tradition never sleeps.
Boise State.db: 4-2-5 fast — the G5's most talented defense.
Boise State.dt: Top-25-caliber front; the blue turf standard holds.

Fresno State.ob: Matt Entz's physical program — FCS-dynasty structure in year two.
Fresno State.ot: Run-first identity, QB competition; the Valley talent base delivers.

Hawai'i.ob: Timmy Chang's run-and-shoot revival — Micah Alejado's breakout made it real.
Hawai'i.ot: The island's system is back: four-wide, choice routes, volume. Alejado is the G5's most fun watch.
Hawai'i.dt: They'll trade scores; the defense needs three stops, not ten.

Nevada.ob: Jeff Choate's toughness rebuild — year three of the climb.
Nevada.ot: Run-heavy, defense-first; wins inch upward annually.
Nevada.dt: Respectable now; explosive-thin offenses keep games ugly by design.

New Mexico.ob: Jason Eck's option-spread — the 2025 breakout (+15.1 FPI residual, market never caught up).
New Mexico.ot: Our model flagged them as the year's most underrated G5 team and the season validated it; the QB-run game returns its core.
New Mexico.dt: Improved to league-median; the offense carries.

San Diego State.ob: Sean Lewis' tempo spread — year two of pace in a program built on defense.
San Diego State.ot: The offense modernized; the QB room remains a competition.
San Diego State.dt: Top-third MW annually; the Aztec defensive standard survives coordinators.

San José State.ob: Ken Niumatalolo's option-influenced evolution — pass-heavier than his Navy roots, year three.
San José State.ot: Balanced attack around a veteran OL; the Spartans hover at bowl-line annually.

UNLV.ob: Dan Mullen's spread — year two, the Vegas project has real money and real expectations now.
UNLV.ot: Mullen's QB development resume meets a portal-built roster; 2025 delivered again and the bar is now league titles.
UNLV.dt: Havoc-rich, bust-prone; they out-athlete the league's middle.

Utah State.ob: Bronco Mendenhall's program discipline — year two of the culture transplant.
Utah State.ot: Run-first pragmatism, QB competition, penalty-free football.
Utah State.dt: Median MW, trending sound; the rebuild is ahead of schedule.

Wyoming.ob: Jay Sawvel's defense-first program — Laramie football as always.
Wyoming.ot: Ground game and play-action; the altitude and physicality do the rest.
Wyoming.dt: Top-third MW defense; offense's job is not to lose.

App State.ob: Dowell Loggains' pro-spread — the Boone standard rebuilding after two mid years.
App State.ot: NFL-tree QB coaching with a physical run base; the roster returns solid production.
App State.dt: Median Sun Belt now; the standard is higher and everyone in Boone knows it.

Arkansas State.ob: Butch Jones' veteran build — quietly back to bowl-level.
Arkansas State.dt: Competent; wins the games it should.

Coastal Carolina.ob: Ryan Beard (from Missouri State's rise) — young HC energy for the post-Chadwell drift.
Coastal Carolina.ot: The spread-option DNA remains in the roster; Beard's task is re-sharpening an identity that dulled.
Coastal Carolina.dt: The -8.8 residual flagged 2025's market overrating; honest reset year.

Georgia Southern.ob: Clay Helton's pro-style — steady bowl-level program.
Georgia Southern.ot: Veteran QB play annually; the option ghosts are long gone.
Georgia Southern.dt: Middle of the league both sides; special teams above.

Georgia State.ob: Dell McGee's ground-first rebuild — year three grind.
Georgia State.ot: Atlanta talent, run-heavy identity, developing QB room.

James Madison.ob: Billy Napier lands at the G5's best program — inheriting the roster Chesney took to the brink of the CFP.
James Madison.ot: Napier's process-driven pro-spread meets JMU's absurd G5 talent level; the fit is cleaner than Gainesville ever was.
James Madison.db: 4-2-5 loaded — the Sun Belt's best unit returns its core.
James Madison.dt: The +13.2 residual flagged them as market-underrated in 2025 and the CFP push proved it.
s: Best G5 roster east of Boise

Louisiana.ob: Desormeaux's option-influenced spread — the Cajuns' physical identity, retooling year.
Louisiana.ot: The -10.9 residual said the market overrated 2025; graduations across both lines make this a legitimate reset.
Louisiana.dt: League-median at best while the two-deep regrows.
w: Sun Belt West rising around them

Marshall.ob: Tony Gibson's defense-first program — year two after the title-game run.
Marshall.ot: Run-heavy, physical, QB-stable; the Herd's identity is set.
Marshall.db: 3-3-5 attacking — Gibson's specialty and the league's most disruptive front.
Marshall.dt: Top-third G5 havoc; they break offenses that lack answers vs pressure.

Old Dominion.ob: Rahne's spread around Colton Joseph — the league's best returning dual-threat.
Old Dominion.ot: Joseph's +11.9-residual-flagged 2025 (market never caught up) returns; the offense is the league's most QB-centric.
Old Dominion.dt: Good enough behind Joseph; not good enough without him.

South Alabama.ob: Major Applewhite's balanced spread — steady, veteran, bowl-level.
South Alabama.ot: The -9.3 residual suggested market overrating in 2025; honest mid-tier now.

Southern Miss.ob: Blake Anderson promoted after Huff's Memphis exit — continuity for the league's fastest one-year turnaround.
Southern Miss.ot: The roster Huff rebuilt returns substantial production; Anderson's own HC resume (Arkansas State, USU) eases the handoff.
Southern Miss.dt: Top-half league after being bottom-five in 2024 — the flip was real.

Texas State.ob: GJ Kinne's tempo spread — the league's most explosive offense, now with expectations (and a new conference? verify Pac-12 move timing).
Texas State.ot: Kinne's system prints yards; the program's trajectory is the Sun Belt's steepest.
Texas State.dt: Median and climbing; the offense buys margin.
s: Kinne's offense elite for the level

Troy.ob: Gerad Parker's physical program — the Trojans' defensive identity persists.
Troy.ot: Run-first offense, veteran defense; November football wins the division historically.
Troy.dt: Top-third annually regardless of staff; it's cultural.

North Dakota State.ob: The FCS dynasty goes FBS — power run game, TE-heavy, the winningest program model in college football (verify 2026 staff).
North Dakota State.ot: The Bison bring championship infrastructure, not transition chaos; the roster out-executes its new league immediately even if athleticism lags.
North Dakota State.dt: No FPI prior exists; treat market lines on them with humility either way.
w: No ratings prior — model blind spot

Sacramento State.ob: FBS newcomer with aggressive NIL ambition — the boldest transition bet of the cycle (verify staff).
Sacramento State.ot: The program spent like an FBS builder before arriving; roster quality is genuinely unknown against this level.
Sacramento State.dt: No prior data at this level; September tells all.
w: Model has no prior — max uncertainty

UConn.ob: Jason Candle (from Toledo, after Mora's Colorado State exit) — his veteran MAC efficiency machine comes to independence.
UConn.ot: Candle's Toledo teams were perennially efficient with plug-in QBs; UConn's independence scheduling gives him a bowl-math-friendly runway.
UConn.db: 4-2-5, structured — Candle's programs defend competently, always.
UConn.dt: Mora left the program healthier than he found it; Candle's floor is higher than the fanbase's memory. The rent-a-schedule keeps ceilings modest.
s: Candle is a proven program CEO

UL Monroe.ob: The league's hardest job — perennial rebuild, new energy annually (verify staff).
UL Monroe.ot: Roster assembled from portal overflow; competitive quarters are the metric.
UL Monroe.dt: Bottom-of-FBS metrics; every win is coaching.

Colorado State.ob: Jim Mora (from UConn) replaces Jay Norvell — pro-style structure and NFL-tree discipline come to Fort Collins.
Colorado State.ot: Mora rebuilt UConn from irrelevance to bowls; CSU hands him a better talent base and the rebuilt Pac-12 stage. QB room opens to competition.
Colorado State.db: 4-3 NFL-structured — Mora defenses tackle and align immediately.
Colorado State.dt: The -8.9 FPI residual flagged 2025 as market-overrated, and the staff change confirms the reset. Floor rises fast under Mora; ceilings take longer.
s: Mora is a proven program resuscitator
Pac-12 move raises the stakes and the revenue
